verses.text: The word of Samuel came to all Israel. Now Israel went out against the Philistines to battle, and pitched beside Eben-ezer: and the Philistines pitched in Aphek. The Philistines put themselves in array against Israel: and when they joined battle, Israel was smitten before the Philistines: and they slew of the army in the field about four thousand men. Guidance: 'The word of Samuel came to all Israel.' Then immediately a war that Samuel apparently did not order. Israel marches out against the Philistines without consulting the new prophet. The chapter opens with the contrast: Israel has a word from God again, but they have not learned to ask the word before they ask the sword. Four thousand fall on the first day at Eben-ezer ('stone of help' — a name that will become deeply ironic when Samuel finally names a victory stone there in chapter 7). Battle started without prayer ends with bodies.

The word of Samuel came to all Israel. Now Israel went out against the Philistines to battle, and pitched beside Eben-ezer: and the Philistines pitched in Aphek. The Philistines put themselves in array against Israel: and when they joined battle, Israel was smitten before the Philistines: and they slew of the army in the field about four thousand men. Guidance: 'The word of Samuel came to all Israel.' Then immediately a war that Samuel apparently did not order. Israel marches out against the Philistines without consulting the new prophet. The chapter opens with the contrast: Israel has a word from God again, but they have not learned to ask the word before they ask the sword. Four thousand fall on the first day at Eben-ezer ('stone of help' — a name that will become deeply ironic when Samuel finally names a victory stone there in chapter 7). Battle started without prayer ends with bodies.

When the people were come into the camp, the elders of Israel said, Wherefore hath the LORD smitten us today before the Philistines? Let us fetch the ark of the covenant of the LORD out of Shiloh unto us, that, when it cometh among us, it may save us out of the hand of our enemies. So the people sent to Shiloh, that they might bring from thence the ark of the covenant of the LORD of hosts, which dwelleth between the cherubims: and the two sons of Eli, Hophni and Phinehas, were there with the ark of the covenant of God. Guidance: The elders ask the right question — 'wherefore hath the LORD smitten us?' — but they bypass any inquiry of Samuel and head straight for the ark. The instinct is superstitious: treat the ark like a magic wand. 'When it cometh among us, it may save us' — they want the object, not the God of the object. And they pick Hophni and Phinehas as carriers, the very priests whose sins triggered God's judgment. The chapter is the painful schooling: Israel still thinks the LORD's presence can be summoned by ritual hardware. The next paragraph is His correction.

When the people were come into the camp, the elders of Israel said, Wherefore hath the LORD smitten us today before the Philistines? Let us fetch the ark of the covenant of the LORD out of Shiloh unto us, that, when it cometh among us, it may save us out of the hand of our enemies. So the people sent to Shiloh, that they might bring from thence the ark of the covenant of the LORD of hosts, which dwelleth between the cherubims: and the two sons of Eli, Hophni and Phinehas, were there with the ark of the covenant of God. Guidance: The elders ask the right question — 'wherefore hath the LORD smitten us?' — but they bypass any inquiry of Samuel and head straight for the ark. The instinct is superstitious: treat the ark like a magic wand. 'When it cometh among us, it may save us' — they want the object, not the God of the object. And they pick Hophni and Phinehas as carriers, the very priests whose sins triggered God's judgment. The chapter is the painful schooling: Israel still thinks the LORD's presence can be summoned by ritual hardware. The next paragraph is His correction.

When the ark of the covenant of the LORD came into the camp, all Israel shouted with a great shout, so that the earth rang again. When the Philistines heard the noise of the shout, they said, What meaneth the noise of this great shout in the camp of the Hebrews? And they understood that the ark of the LORD was come into the camp. Guidance: The ark arrives, the army cheers, the ground reverberates. Israel is celebrating their assumed victory before any sword has swung. The Philistines hear the shout and figure out what has happened. Pagans often recognize the spiritual mechanics of religion better than the religious do — at least they take the ark seriously enough to be terrified. Israel's noise is louder than its faith.

When the Philistines heard the noise of the shout, they said, What meaneth the noise of this great shout in the camp of the Hebrews? And they understood that the ark of the LORD was come into the camp. Guidance: The ark arrives, the army cheers, the ground reverberates. Israel is celebrating their assumed victory before any sword has swung. The Philistines hear the shout and figure out what has happened. Pagans often recognize the spiritual mechanics of religion better than the religious do — at least they take the ark seriously enough to be terrified. Israel's noise is louder than its faith.

The Philistines were afraid, for they said, God is come into the camp. And they said, Woe unto us! for there hath not been such a thing heretofore. Woe unto us! who shall deliver us out of the hand of these mighty Gods? these are the Gods that smote the Egyptians with all the plagues in the wilderness. Be strong, and quit yourselves like men, O ye Philistines, that ye be not servants unto the Hebrews, as they have been to you: quit yourselves like men, and fight. Guidance: The Philistines' theology is muddled — 'these mighty Gods,' plural, the polytheistic instinct — but their fear is accurate. They remember the exodus stories from across the centuries. Yet fear converts to resolve: 'quit yourselves like men.' Sometimes the enemy of God's people takes their God more seriously than they do, and that very seriousness becomes a weapon. Pagan courage shaped by terror often beats covenant complacency wrapped in noise. The cheering Israelite army will lose to the trembling Philistine one.

The Philistines fought, and Israel was smitten, and they fled every man into his tent: and there was a very great slaughter; for there fell of Israel thirty thousand footmen. The ark of God was taken; and the two sons of Eli, Hophni and Phinehas, were slain. Guidance: Thirty thousand. The single worst military defeat in Israel's history up to this point. And the verse drops the bombshell almost dryly: 'the ark of God was taken.' Israel has lost its most sacred object to pagans. And the same day fulfills 2:34 — Hophni and Phinehas die. The LORD's prophecy to Eli now has a date stamp. The ark was supposed to be the trump card; it became the spoils of war. God refused to be reduced to a weapon, even by His own people. He let His ark be carried away rather than be used as a charm.

The Philistines fought, and Israel was smitten, and they fled every man into his tent: and there was a very great slaughter; for there fell of Israel thirty thousand footmen. The ark of God was taken; and the two sons of Eli, Hophni and Phinehas, were slain. Guidance: Thirty thousand. The single worst military defeat in Israel's history up to this point. And the verse drops the bombshell almost dryly: 'the ark of God was taken.' Israel has lost its most sacred object to pagans. And the same day fulfills 2:34 — Hophni and Phinehas die. The LORD's prophecy to Eli now has a date stamp. The ark was supposed to be the trump card; it became the spoils of war. God refused to be reduced to a weapon, even by His own people. He let His ark be carried away rather than be used as a charm.

There ran a man of Benjamin out of the army, and came to Shiloh the same day with his clothes rent, and with earth upon his head. When he came, lo, Eli sat upon a seat by the wayside watching: for his heart trembled for the ark of God. When the man came into the city, and told it, all the city cried out. Guidance: A Benjamite runner — fast, tribal, traditional sign of bad news (rent clothes, earth on head). And the chapter's most poignant detail: Eli is sitting by the road, waiting. 'His heart trembled for the ark of God.' Eli got the prophecies of judgment; he could have run for the ark himself. But ninety-eight years and blind, he sits, posting himself like a sentinel for news he knows will be bad. The chapter quietly honors that — at least at the end, Eli's heart was for the right thing. The city erupts in grief before the priest even hears the words.

There ran a man of Benjamin out of the army, and came to Shiloh the same day with his clothes rent, and with earth upon his head. When he came, lo, Eli sat upon a seat by the wayside watching: for his heart trembled for the ark of God. When the man came into the city, and told it, all the city cried out. Guidance: A Benjamite runner — fast, tribal, traditional sign of bad news (rent clothes, earth on head). And the chapter's most poignant detail: Eli is sitting by the road, waiting. 'His heart trembled for the ark of God.' Eli got the prophecies of judgment; he could have run for the ark himself. But ninety-eight years and blind, he sits, posting himself like a sentinel for news he knows will be bad. The chapter quietly honors that — at least at the end, Eli's heart was for the right thing. The city erupts in grief before the priest even hears the words.

When Eli heard the noise of the crying, he said, What meaneth the noise of this tumult? And the man came in hastily, and told Eli. Now Eli was ninety and eight years old; and his eyes were dim, that he could not see. Guidance: Eli, blind, only hears the city's cry. He asks 'what meaneth this tumult?' — almost the same words the Philistines asked when they heard Israel's shout (v6). Both questions go ungranted; both armies' assumptions get exposed. Ninety-eight years; the priesthood is about to change hands by force of mortality. The bookends of the chapter: a national shout of confidence, a city wail of disaster, both heard and misread before the truth lands.

Now Eli was ninety and eight years old; and his eyes were dim, that he could not see. Guidance: Eli, blind, only hears the city's cry. Ninety-eight years; the priesthood is about to change hands by force of mortality. The bookends of the chapter: a national shout of confidence, a city wail of disaster, both heard and misread before the truth lands.

The man said unto Eli, I am he that came out of the army, and I fled today out of the army. And he said, What is there done, my son? And the messenger answered and said, Israel is fled before the Philistines, and there hath been also a great slaughter among the people, and thy two sons also, Hophni and Phinehas, are dead, and the ark of God is taken. It came to pass, when he made mention of the ark of God, that he fell from off the seat backward by the side of the gate, and his neck brake, and he died: for he was an old man, and heavy. And he had judged Israel forty years. Guidance: Four pieces of news, in escalating weight: Israel fled, many slain, your sons dead, the ark is taken. The fourth is what kills Eli. He has heard the news about his sons — and he stays in his seat. The mention of the ark is what tips him backward. For all his faults, his last functioning grief is for the ark, not the family. He literally falls under the weight of the news. 'Heavy' — the same Hebrew root as the word for 'glory' (kavod) — the irony is built into the language. The man who failed to give weight to God's commands now collapses under the weight of God's discipline. Forty years of judging end at a gate.

His daughter in law, Phinehas' wife, was with child, near to be delivered: and when she heard the tidings that the ark of God was taken, and that her father in law and her husband were dead, she bowed herself and travailed; for her pains came upon her. About the time of her death the women that stood by her said unto her, Fear not; for thou hast born a son. But she answered not, neither did she regard it. She named the child I-chabod, saying, The glory is departed from Israel: because the ark of God was taken, and because of her father in law and her husband. And she said, The glory is departed from Israel: for the ark of God is taken. Guidance: The chapter's saddest scene. Phinehas' wife is in labor. The news hits her in three places at once: husband, father-in-law, ark. The shock induces labor and she dies bearing a son. The midwives' kindest words — 'thou hast born a son' — do not register. Her last act is to name him: 'Ichabod' (i-kavod, 'no glory' or 'where is the glory?'). She does not name him for her dead husband or her father-in-law's house; she names him for the LORD's departure. The narrator emphasizes 'because the ark of God was taken' — twice. Her grief is theological, not domestic. She knew what mattered. The chapter that began with Israel cheering the ark ends with a dying woman naming the loss of God's glory more accurately than the entire army did.
